Market Note  /  July 18, 2026

Buying Into the Bloodbath

What's actually driving this selloff, why the fundamentals underneath it tell a different story than the price action, and the three positions added into the pullback.

My portfolio's down $12,000 this week, and honestly, I'm not stressed. Here's what's actually causing the dump, and why the fundamentals underneath it are telling a completely different story than the price action.

Zoom out and the headline number is almost boring: the S&P 500 is only down about 1.5–1.6% on the week. That's nothing — a normal week of noise, not a crisis. The real damage is one sector.

−20% The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX), in three weeks — a technical bear market, and the actual story behind this "crash."

A 20%-plus move in one sector while the broad market barely wobbles tells you this is a rotation and a narrative shock, not a broad economic downturn. That distinction matters for everything that follows.

·  ·  ·

Why it's actually dropping

Iran & the Strait of Hormuz

A ceasefire between the US and Iran, formalized June 17, broke down in early July after Iran struck commercial vessels in the Strait — one of the most important oil chokepoints on earth. Iran has since declared the Strait closed; the US has responded with strikes and reasserted naval posture in the region.

Oil had actually spent June grinding back down toward $70 as the ceasefire held. The July break sent it snapping right back the other way.

+5% Brent crude, in a single session — a real external shock, not sentiment. The one variable here that could genuinely bleed into inflation if it drags on.

Kimi K3 — the bigger driver for tech

On July 16, Chinese lab Moonshot released Kimi K3, a 2.8-trillion-parameter open-weight model — the largest open-source model ever built — and independent benchmarks show it closing in on the very top US labs.

2.8T parameters · released July 16 · open-weight · ranks #4 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index · took #1 on the Frontend Code Arena leaderboard, scoring 1,679 against Claude Fable 5's 1,631.

For a lab that was left for dead after DeepSeek's rise, that's a genuinely loud result. Traders are calling it "DeepSeek moment 2.0" — sell AI infrastructure on the theory that a cheaper, open model competing with the frontier means less US compute spend is needed. This is where I disagree with the market.

The counter-read

Efficiency doesn't shrink the AI trade — it expands it. Cheaper, more accessible models mean more builders, and more builders means more infrastructure demand, not less. DeepSeek didn't kill the AI trade in 2025; it accelerated it. The market looks like it's replaying the same mistake.

·  ·  ·

What's getting buried under the noise

While headlines chase Iran and Kimi K3, the actual fundamental data coming in right now tells a completely different story.

SignalReadingContext
Bank earnings+20–45%EPS beats at JPM, GS, TRV this week — margin expansion, not compression
June CPI3.5%Down from a 4.2% May peak; largest one-month drop since April 2020
Fed funds3.50–3.75%Held steady in June; no hikes signaled for July 29

Put together: the market is selling off on a story about the future of AI compute demand, while the current earnings and inflation data is about as good as it's looked all year. That gap — between the narrative and the numbers — is exactly where I've been adding.

·  ·  ·

Where I'm putting money

Roundhill Memory ETF DRAM

$52.72 ▲ 0.7%
52-week range$26.14 – $81.34
50 / 200-day avg.$61.56 / $53.41
Off Jun 22 peak−35%
Since Apr 2 launch+88%
Top holdingsMU / Samsung / SK Hynix, ~73%

Not a Micron-only bet — nine holdings, but concentrated, with real Korean-won currency exposure via Samsung and SK Hynix. Logic chips are getting hammered on compute-demand panic, but DRAM contract prices are still rising 13–18% quarter-over-quarter, and AI datacenters are on pace to eat roughly 70% of high-end DRAM output this year. That side of the story isn't priced in yet.

Risk. Memory is historically one of the most cyclical corners of semiconductors; prior cycles have seen 40–60% give-backs within six months of a pricing peak. The thesis rests entirely on DRAM/HBM pricing not rolling over.

Meta Platforms META

$646.01 ▼ 2.8%
52-week range$520.26 – $796.25
50 / 200-day avg.$604.54 / $640.51
Consensus target$827.50 (+28%)
Lowest Street target$700
Ratings, buy/hold/sell52 / 11 / 2

Reportedly in early talks to lease up to $10B in compute to Anthropic over two years — a real second revenue line on top of the AI buildout, similar to what AWS became for Amazon.

Nothing signed yet. The stock dipped on news Meta hired a 19-year AWS veteran to build that cloud arm, which the market read as more near-term capex, not future revenue. That's a timing mismatch, not a fundamentals problem — and even the Street's lowest target sits above today's price.

Credo Technology CRDO

$202.68 ▼ 2.5%
52-week range$86.49 – $308.67
50 / 200-day avg.$228.95 / $162.84
Off 52-week high−34%
Consensus target$270.10 (+33%)
Ratings, buy/hold/sell13 / 2 / 0

The most direct, highest-torque play on this whole thesis — Credo builds the high-speed connectivity hyperscalers need to physically wire AI datacenters together. That's exactly why it moves hardest in both directions when the capex narrative swings, and why it's had the roughest week of the three. Analyst sentiment here is the cleanest of the group: zero sell ratings.

·  ·  ·

The bottom line

None of this means the selloff isn't real, or that these stocks can't keep falling in the near term — a narrative shock doesn't need to be correct to keep moving prices for a while. But when I weigh what's actually driving the tape against what the underlying fundamentals show, I see a gap between story and substance. That gap is where I'm choosing to add, not sit on the sidelines.
